file.content:
Package: neurosurf
Type: Package
Title: Data structures and visualization for surface-based neuroimaging data
Version: 0.1.0
Authors@R: c(
    person(c("Bradley", "R"), "Buchsbaum", role = c("aut", "cre"), email = "brad.buchsbaum@gmail.com"))
Maintainer: Bradley Buchsbaum <brad.buchsbaum@gmail.com>
Description: A comprehensive toolkit for working with surface-based neuroimaging data
    represented as triangle meshes. The package provides classes and methods for 
    creating, manipulating, and visualizing 3D surface geometries (e.g., cortical 
    surfaces), with support for various file formats including FreeSurfer and GIFTI. 
    Key features include: surface smoothing, curvature computation, neighborhood graph 
    construction, geodesic distance calculations, searchlight analysis for surface-based 
    machine learning, and interactive 3D visualization via HTMLWidgets. The package 
    facilitates advanced surface-based analyses through specialized data structures 
    for representing surface geometry and associated functional data.
Imports:
    Matrix,
    igraph,
    rgl,
    assertthat,
    neuroim2,
    colorplane,
    FNN,
    readr,
    stringr,
    plyr,
    Rvcg,
    methods,
    gplots,
    gifti,
    htmlwidgets,
    deflist,
    crayon
License: GPL (>= 2)
LazyData: TRUE
RoxygenNote: 7.3.2.9000
Remotes:
  bbuchsbaum/neuroim2,
  bbuchsbaum/colorplane
Collate:
    'all_generic.R'
    'all_class.R'
    'Arith.R'
    'IO.R'
    'ROI.R'
    'Searchlight.R'
    'curv.R'
    'geometry.R'
    'neighborhood.R'
    'neuro_surface.R'
    'neurosurf.R'
    'spec.R'
    'surfwidget.R'
    'view_surface.R'
    'vol_to_surf.R'
    'zzz.R'
Suggests: 
    testthat,
    covr,
    knitr,
    rmarkdown
URL: https://github.com/bbuchsbaum/neurosurf
BugReports: https://github.com/bbuchsbaum/neurosurf/issues
VignetteBuilder: knitr
Encoding: UTF-8

#' Construct Neighborhood Graph from Surface Mesh
#'
#' @description
#' This generic function constructs a neighborhood graph from a surface mesh using edge weights.
#' It allows for flexible definition of neighborhoods based on edge radius and custom edge weights.
#'
#' @param x An object representing a surface mesh. The specific class depends on the method implementation.
#' @param radius Numeric. The edge radius defining the neighborhood extent.
#' @param edgeWeights Numeric vector. Custom weights for edges, used to define edge distances.
#' @param nodes Integer vector. The subset of nodes to use in graph construction. If NULL, all nodes are used.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to methods.
#'
#' @return An object representing the constructed neighborhood graph. The specific class depends on the method implementation.
#'
#' @details
#' The neighborhood graph is constructed by considering edges within the specified radius
#' and applying the provided edge weights. This function is particularly useful in
#' neuroimaging analyses for defining local connectivity on brain surfaces.
#'
#' @examples
#' \donttest{
#' # Assuming 'surface_mesh' is a pre-defined surface mesh object
#' graph <- neighbor_graph(surface_mesh, radius = 5,
#'                         edgeWeights = runif(nrow(surface_mesh$vertices)),
#'                         nodes = 1:1000)
#' }
#'
#' @seealso \code{\link{graph}}, \code{\link{vertices}}
#'
#' @export
#' @rdname neighbor_graph-methods
setGeneric(name = "neighbor_graph",
           def = function(x, radius, edgeWeights=missing(), nodes=missing(), ...)

# neurosurf

Under development

## Installation

You can install neurosurf from github with:

``` r
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("bbuchsbaum/neurosurf")
```

## Vignettes

See examples of use of `neurosurf` in the

[vignettes](https://bbuchsbaum.github.io/neurosurf/articles/index.html).

## Curvature shading

`surfwidget()` includes an optional `curvature` argument supplying per vertex
curvature values. When omitted and the input is a `SurfaceGeometry` object the
values are computed via `curvature(x)` and forwarded to the viewer for
curvature based coloring.

#' surfwidget
#'
#' Create a surfwidget to display brain surface data.
#'
#' @param x A SurfaceGeometry, NeuroSurface, ColorMappedNeuroSurface, or VertexColoredNeuroSurface object
#' @param width The width of the widget
#' @param height The height of the widget
#' @param data Optional. Numeric vector of data values for each vertex.
#' @param cmap Optional. Color map for data visualization.
#' @param irange Optional. Intensity range for data visualization.
#' @param thresh Optional. Threshold range for data visualization.
#' @param vertexColors Optional. Vector of colors for each vertex.
#' @param alpha Opacity of the surface (0 to 1).
#' @param curvature Optional numeric vector of curvature values for each vertex.
#'   If not supplied for a \code{SurfaceGeometry} object, it is computed via
#'   \code{curvature(x)}.
#' @param config A list of configuration options for the surface rendering:
#'   \itemize{
#'     \item{\code{shininess}}{Numeric between 0 and 100. Controls the shininess of the material. Higher values create a more polished appearance. Default is 30.}
#'     \item{\code{specularColor}}{Character. Hex color code for the specular highlights. Default is "#111111".}
#'     \item{\code{flatShading}}{Logical scalar. If \code{TRUE}, uses flat shading; if \code{FALSE}, uses smooth shading. Default is \code{FALSE}.}
#'     \item{\code{ambientLightColor}}{Character. Hex color code for the ambient light. Default is "#404040".}
#'     \item{\code{directionalLightColor}}{Character. Hex color code for the directional light. Default is "#ffffff".}
#'     \item{\code{directionalLightIntensity}}{Numeric between 0 and 1. Intensity of the directional light. Default is 0.5.}
#'   }
#'   Unknown elements in \code{config} are ignored with a warning.
#'
#' @import htmlwidgets
#' @importFrom grDevices col2rgb rgb rainbow
#'
#' @return An HTMLWidget object
#' @rdname surfwidget-methods
#' @export
setMethod("surfwidget", signature(x = "SurfaceGeometry"),
  function(x, width = NULL, height = NULL, data = NULL, cmap = grDevices::rainbow(256),
           irange = NULL, thresh = c(0,0), vertexColors = NULL, alpha = 1,
           curvature = NULL, config = list(), ...) {

    curv_vals <- curvature
    if (is.null(curv_vals)) {
      curv_vals <- curvature(x)
    }

    # Extract data values if not provided
    if (is.null(data)) {
      data <- curvature(x)
    }

    # Create a NeuroSurface and call its method
    neuro_surface <- NeuroSurface(x, indices=nodes(x), data)

## Summary of the neurosurf R Package Structure and Components

Based on my exploration of the neurosurf package, here's a comprehensive summary:

### Package Overview
**neurosurf** is a comprehensive toolkit for working with surface-based neuroimaging data represented as triangle meshes. It's designed for creating, manipulating, and visualizing 3D surface geometries (particularly cortical surfaces).

### Key Package Metadata
- **Version**: 0.1.0
- **Author**: Bradley R. Buchsbaum
- **License**: GPL (>= 2)
- **Purpose**: Surface-based neuroimaging data analysis and visualization

### Core Dependencies
The package relies on several key packages:
- **rgl**: 3D visualization using OpenGL
- **igraph**: Graph/network operations for surface connectivity
- **Rvcg**: Surface geometry operations (smoothing, remeshing)
- **gifti**: GIFTI file format support
- **htmlwidgets**: Interactive web-based visualizations
- **neuroim2**: Core neuroimaging data structures
- **colorplane**: Color mapping utilities

### Package Structure

#### 1. **Source Files (R/)**
- **Core Classes** (`all_class.R`): Defines main S4 classes for surface data structures
- **Generics** (`all_generic.R`): Generic function definitions
- **IO Operations** (`IO.R`): File reading/writing for various formats (FreeSurfer, GIFTI, AFNI NIML)
- **Geometry** (`geometry.R`): Surface geometry operations
- **Visualization**:
  - `view_surface.R`: RGL-based 3D visualization
  - `surfwidget.R`: HTMLWidget-based interactive visualization
- **Analysis Tools**:
  - `Searchlight.R`: Surface-based searchlight analysis
  - `neighborhood.R`: Neighborhood graph construction
  - `ROI.R`: Region of interest operations
  - `curv.R`: Curvature computations
- **Utilities**:
  - `vol_to_surf.R`: Volume to surface mapping
  - `Arith.R`: Arithmetic operations
  - `smooth.R`: Surface smoothing operations

#### 2. **Main Classes Exported**
- `SurfaceGeometry`: Base class for surface mesh representation
- `NeuroSurface`: Surface with associated data values
- `ColorMappedNeuroSurface`: Surface with color mapping
- `VertexColoredNeuroSurface`: Surface with per-vertex colors
- `ROISurface`: Surface regions of interest
- `SurfaceSearchlight`: Surface-based searchlight analysis

#### 3. **Supported File Formats**
- FreeSurfer surface files (ASCII and binary)
- GIFTI (.gii) format
- AFNI NIML datasets
- Various annotation and data formats

#### 4. **Visualization Capabilities**

**RGL Integration**:
- Used for OpenGL-based 3D rendering
- Functions: `shade3d`, `spheres3d`, `view3d`
- Interactive rotation and zoom
- Surface normals computation

**HTMLWidgets Integration**:
- Web-based interactive visualization
- JavaScript/Three.js based rendering
- Configurable lighting, materials, and shading
- Support for curvature-based coloring

#### 5. **Test Suite**
Located in `tests/testthat/`:
- Connection/graph tests
- Data reader tests
- Neighborhood computation tests
- I/O operations tests
- Searchlight analysis tests
- Surface smoothing tests

#### 6. **Vignettes**
Three main vignettes:
- `introduction-to-neurosurf.Rmd`: Basic package introduction
- `displaying-surfaces.Rmd`: Surface visualization guide
- `interactive-surfaces.Rmd`: Interactive visualization features

#### 7. **Data Resources**
The `inst/extdata/` directory contains:
- Example surface files (FreeSurfer format)
- GIFTI format examples
- NIML datasets
- Parcellation data (Schaefer atlases)
- Test data for various formats

#### 8. **Experimental Features**
An `experimental/` directory suggests ongoing development of new features.

### Key Functionality Highlights

1. **Surface Representation**: Comprehensive S4 class system for representing surface meshes with associated data
2. **File I/O**: Support for major neuroimaging surface formats
3. **Geometry Operations**: Smoothing, remeshing, curvature computation
4. **Graph Operations**: Neighborhood graphs, geodesic distances, connectivity analysis
5. **Visualization**: Both static (rgl) and interactive (htmlwidgets) 3D visualization
6. **Analysis Tools**: Surface-based searchlight for machine learning applications
7. **Integration**: Works with neuroim2 for broader neuroimaging workflows

The package appears to be a mature, well-structured toolkit for surface-based neuroimaging analysis with strong visualization capabilities and support for standard file formats used in the field.

    52→#' This function maps a vector of surface curvature values (e.g., mean curvature)

    61→#' @param outcol A character string specifying the hex color code to represent

    68→#' @details

    70→#' Mean curvature is often used, where positive values typically indicate outward
    71→#' curvature (gyri) and negative values indicate inward curvature (sulci).
    72→#' This function simplifies the curvature map into two colors based on whether
    73→#' the value is above or below the median curvature, providing a quick visual

    75→#' to values *above* the median and `outcol` to values *at or below* the median.

    92→#'

    95→#' @export

#' Convert Curvature Values to Binary Colors for Visualization
#'
#' @description
#' This function maps a vector of surface curvature values (e.g., mean curvature)
#' to a binary color scheme, typically used to distinguish gyri (outward folds)
#' from sulci (inward folds) on a brain surface visualization.
#'
#' @param vals A numeric vector containing curvature values for each vertex
#'   on the surface.
#' @param incol A character string specifying the hex color code to represent
#'   vertices with curvature values *greater than* the median curvature.
#'   Default is "#B3B3B3" (light gray).
#' @param outcol A character string specifying the hex color code to represent
#'   vertices with curvature values *less than or equal to* the median curvature.
#'   Default is "#404040" (dark gray).
#'
#' @return A character vector of the same length as `vals`, containing hex color
#'   codes based on the binary classification of curvature values relative to the median.
#'
#' @details
#' Surface curvature provides information about the local shape of the surface.
#' Mean curvature is often used, where positive values typically indicate outward
#' curvature (gyri) and negative values indicate inward curvature (sulci).
#' This function simplifies the curvature map into two colors based on whether
#' the value is above or below the median curvature, providing a quick visual
#' distinction between these features. Note the default coloring assigns `incol`
#' to values *above* the median and `outcol` to values *at or below* the median.
#' You might need to adjust `incol` and `outcol` depending on the specific
#' interpretation of curvature values in your data (e.g., if positive values
#' represent sulci).
#'
#' @examples
#' # Generate some example curvature values
#' set.seed(123)
#' curvature_values <- rnorm(100, mean = 0, sd = 0.1)
#'
#' # Get binary colors using default light/dark gray
#' gray_colors <- curv_cols(curvature_values)
#' table(gray_colors)
#'
#' # Use different colors (e.g., red for above median, blue for below)
#' red_blue_colors <- curv_cols(curvature_values, incol = "#FF0000", outcol = "#0000FF")
#' table(red_blue_colors)
#'
#' @seealso \code{\link{curvature}}, \code{\link{view_surface}}
#'
#' @export
curv_cols <- function(vals, incol="#B3B3B3", outcol="#404040") {
  ifelse(vals > stats::median(vals), incol, outcol)
}

#' Display a 3D Brain Surface using RGL
#'
#' @description
#' Renders a 3D brain surface mesh using the `rgl` package. This function provides
#' flexible options for coloring the surface based on data values or predefined
#' colors, adjusting transparency, controlling lighting, setting viewpoints, and
#' overlaying spherical markers.
#'
#' @param surfgeom A \code{\linkS4class{SurfaceGeometry}} object representing the
#'   3D brain surface mesh to be displayed.
#' @param vals An optional numeric vector containing data values for each vertex
#'   on the surface. If provided and `vert_clrs` is NULL, these values are mapped
#'   to colors using `cmap` and `irange`.
#' @param cmap A vector of colors (e.g., hex codes) defining the color map used
#'   when `vals` is provided and `vert_clrs` is NULL. Defaults to `rainbow(256)`.
#' @param vert_clrs An optional character vector of hex color codes for each vertex.
#'   If provided, these colors directly override any coloring derived from `vals` and `cmap`.
#'   The length should match the number of vertices in `surfgeom`.
#' @param bgcol A single hex color code or a vector of hex color codes used as the
#'   base color for the surface. If `vals` or `vert_clrs` are provided, this color
#'   is blended with the data/vertex colors. Defaults to "lightgray".
#' @param alpha A numeric value between 0 (fully transparent) and 1 (fully opaque)
#'   controlling the overall transparency of the surface. Defaults to 1.
#' @param add_normals Logical. If TRUE (default), surface normals are calculated
#'   and added to the mesh, which improves the appearance of lighting effects.
#' @param thresh An optional numeric vector of length 2, `c(lower, upper)`.
#'   Vertices with `vals` *outside* this range (i.e., `< lower` or `> upper`)
#'   are made fully transparent. This is applied *after* the general `alpha`.
#'   Defaults to NULL (no thresholding).
#' @param irange An optional numeric vector of length 2, `c(min, max)`. Specifies
#'   the range of `vals` to map onto the `cmap`. Values outside this range will be
#'   clamped to the min/max colors. Defaults to the full range of `vals`.
#' @param specular The color of specular highlights on the surface, affecting its
#'   shininess. Can be a color name (e.g., "white") or hex code. Defaults to "white".
#'   Set to "black" or NULL for a matte appearance.
#' @param viewpoint A character string specifying a predefined view (e.g., "lateral",
#'   "medial", "ventral", "posterior"). The actual view depends on the hemisphere
#'   (`surfgeom@hemi`, e.g., "left_lateral"). Alternatively, a 4x4 transformation
#'   matrix defining a custom view. Defaults to "lateral".
#' @param new_window Logical. If TRUE (default), opens a new `rgl` window for the plot.
#'   If FALSE, attempts to plot in the currently active `rgl` window (useful for
#'   updates or within Shiny apps).
#' @param offset A numeric vector of length 3 specifying a translation offset
#'   `c(x, y, z)` applied to the surface coordinates before rendering. Defaults to `c(0, 0, 0)`.
#' @param zoom A numeric value controlling the camera zoom level. Defaults to 1 (no zoom).
#'   Values > 1 zoom in, < 1 zoom out.
#' @param spheres An optional data frame to draw spheres at specific locations on
#'   or near the surface. Must contain columns `x`, `y`, `z` (coordinates), and
#'   `radius`. Can optionally include a `color` column (hex codes or color names)
#'   for individual sphere colors (defaults to black).
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed directly to `rgl::shade3d` for fine-grained
#'   control over rendering (e.g., `lit`, `smooth`).
#'
#' @return Invisibly returns the object ID(s) of the shape(s) added to the RGL scene
#'   by `rgl::shade3d`. This can be useful for modifying the scene later.
#'
#' @details
#' **Coloring:** Surface vertex colors are determined by the following priority:
#'   1. `vert_clrs`: If provided, these specific hex colors are used.
#'   2. `vals` & `cmap`: If `vals` is provided and `vert_clrs` is NULL, `vals` are mapped to `cmap` based on `irange`.
#'   3. `bgcol`: If neither `vert_clrs` nor `vals` are used for coloring, `bgcol` is applied uniformly.
#'   If `bgcol` is specified alongside `vert_clrs` or `vals`, the colors are blended based on the `alpha` parameter.
#'
#' **Transparency:** Overall transparency is set by `alpha`. Additional threshold-based
#'   transparency can be applied using `thresh` when `vals` are provided. Vertices
#'   with values outside the `thresh` range become fully transparent.
#'
#' **Lighting:** `add_normals=TRUE` is recommended for realistic lighting. The `specular`
#'   parameter controls the shininess.
#'
#' **Viewpoint:** Predefined viewpoints (`"lateral"`, `"medial"`, etc.) are automatically
#'   adjusted based on the hemisphere specified in `surfgeom@hemi` (e.g., "lh" results
#'   in "left_lateral"). If `hemi` is unknown, the current `rgl` view is used unless
#'   a custom 4x4 matrix is provided.
#'
#' **Performance:** Rendering very large surfaces or surfaces with complex coloring/transparency
#'   can be computationally intensive.
#'
#' @importFrom gplots col2hex
#' @importFrom rgl open3d clear3d shade3d spheres3d view3d par3d addNormals rgl.useNULL
#' @importFrom colorplane IntensityColorPlane HexColorPlane map_colors blend_colors as_hexcol
#' @importFrom stats median
#' @importFrom grDevices rainbow
#'
#' @examples
#' \donttest{
#' # Assume 'surf_geom' is a SurfaceGeometry object loaded previously
#' # e.g., surf_geom <- read_surf_geometry("path/to/surface.gii")
#'
#' # Simple display with default background color
#' view_surface(surf_geom, viewpoint = "lateral")
#'
#' # Display with curvature coloring (assuming you have curvature data)
#' # curv_vals <- curvature(surf_geom) # Calculate curvature if needed
#' # view_surface(surf_geom, vals = curv_vals, cmap = gray.colors(256), viewpoint = "medial")
#'
#' # Display with specific vertex colors (e.g., based on labels)
#' # num_verts <- length(nodes(surf_geom))
#' # colors_for_verts <- sample(c("red", "blue", "green"), num_verts, replace = TRUE)
#' # view_surface(surf_geom, vert_clrs = colors_for_verts, viewpoint = "ventral")
#'
#' # Display with thresholding and custom color map
#' # random_data <- rnorm(length(nodes(surf_geom)))
#' # view_surface(surf_geom, vals = random_data,
#' #              cmap = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(256),
#' #              thresh = c(-1.5, 1.5), # Make values between -1.5 and 1.5 transparent
#' #              irange = c(-3, 3),     # Map values from -3 to 3 onto the cmap
#' #              viewpoint = "posterior")
#'
#' # Display with spheres marking specific coordinates
#' sphere_coords <- data.frame(
#'   x = c(10, -15, 5),
#'   y = c(20, 0, -10),
#'   z = c(-5, 25, 15),
#'   radius = c(3, 4, 2.5),
#'   color = c("yellow", "cyan", "magenta")
#' )
#' view_surface(surf_geom, viewpoint = "lateral", spheres = sphere_coords)
#'
#' # Plot in the current rgl window without opening a new one
#' # rgl::open3d() # Open window first
#' # view_surface(surf_geom, new_window = FALSE)
#' }
#'
#' @seealso \code{\link[rgl]{shade3d}}, \code{\link[rgl]{spheres3d}}, \code{\link[rgl]{view3d}}, \code{\link{SurfaceGeometry}}
#' @export
view_surface <- function(surfgeom, vals=NA,
                         cmap=grDevices::rainbow(256, alpha = 1),
                         vert_clrs=NULL,
                         bgcol = "lightgray",
                         alpha=1,
                         add_normals=TRUE,
                         thresh=NULL,
                         irange=range(vals,na.rm=TRUE),
                         specular="white",  # Default to white for a shiny surface
                         viewpoint=c("lateral","medial", "ventral", "posterior"),
                         new_window=TRUE,  # New argument to control RGL window
                         offset=c(0,0,0),
                         zoom=1,
                         spheres=NULL,  # New argument for spheres
                         ...) {


  # Open a new rgl window only if not in Shiny
  if (new_window && !rgl::rgl.useNULL()) {
    rgl::open3d()
  } else {
    #rgl::clear3d(type = "all")
  }



  if (add_normals) {
    surfgeom@mesh <- rgl::addNormals(surfgeom@mesh)
  }

  viewpoint <- match.arg(viewpoint)

  umat <- if (is.matrix(viewpoint)) {
    stopifnot(nrow(viewpoint) == 4 && ncol(viewpoint) == 4)
    viewpoint
  } else if (surfgeom@hemi == "lh") {
    viewpoint <- paste0("left_", viewpoint)
    surface_views[[viewpoint]]
  } else if (surfgeom@hemi == "rh") {
    viewpoint <- paste0("right_", viewpoint)
    surface_views[[viewpoint]]
  } else {
    warning("unknown hemisphere, default using viewpoint")
    rgl::par3d()$userMatrix
  }

  if (is.character(bgcol)) {
    bgcol <- gplots::col2hex(bgcol)
  }

  if (length(bgcol) == 1) {
    bg_layer <- colorplane::HexColorPlane(rep(bgcol, length(surfgeom@mesh$vb[1,])))
  } else {
    bg_layer <- colorplane::HexColorPlane(bgcol)

content.text:
Based on my analysis, here's a comprehensive plan for porting neurosurf from R to Python:

## **Python Port Implementation Plan for neurosurf**

### **1. Key Technology Mappings**

| R Component | Python Equivalent | Notes |
|------------|------------------|-------|
| **rgl** (3D visualization) | **vedo** or **pyvista** | Both provide high-level 3D mesh visualization with interactivity |
| **htmlwidgets** | **plotly** or **ipyvolume** | For interactive web-based 3D visualization |
| **igraph** | **networkx** or **igraph-python** | Graph operations for surface connectivity |
| **Rvcg** | **trimesh** + **pymeshlab** | Surface geometry operations |
| **gifti** | **nibabel** | GIFTI file format support |
| **neuroim2** | **nibabel** + custom classes | Core neuroimaging data structures |
| **colorplane** | **matplotlib.colors** + custom | Color mapping utilities |
| **S4 classes** | **dataclasses** + **pydantic** | Type-safe class definitions |

### **2. Recommended Python 3D Visualization Libraries**

After analyzing the rgl usage, I recommend **vedo** as the primary replacement because:
- Direct mesh visualization with `show()` method similar to rgl's `shade3d()`
- Built-in support for vertex colors, transparency, and lighting
- Interactive camera controls matching rgl's `view3d()`
- Sphere rendering for markers (`spheres3d()` → `Sphere()`)
- Better performance for large meshes
- Active development and good documentation

Alternative: **pyvista** (more VTK-like but also excellent)

### **3. Project Structure**

```
pyneurosurf/
├── pyproject.toml
├── README.md
├── pyneurosurf/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── core/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── geometry.py        # SurfaceGeometry class
│   │   ├── surface.py         # NeuroSurface classes
│   │   └── mesh.py            # Mesh operations
│   ├── io/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── freesurfer.py      # FreeSurfer format I/O
│   │   ├── gifti.py           # GIFTI format I/O
│   │   ├── niml.py            # AFNI NIML format I/O
│   │   └── readers.py         # Unified reading interface
│   ├── viz/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── view_surface.py    # 3D visualization with vedo
│   │   ├── plotly_surface.py  # Interactive web visualization
│   │   └── colormaps.py       # Color mapping utilities
│   ├── analysis/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── searchlight.py     # Surface searchlight
│   │   ├── smoothing.py       # Surface smoothing
│   │   ├── curvature.py       # Curvature calculations
│   │   └── roi.py             # ROI operations
│   ├── utils/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── graph.py           # Graph/neighborhood operations
│   │   └── vol_to_surf.py     # Volume to surface mapping
│   └── datasets/
│       └── __init__.py        # Example data loaders
├── tests/
│   ├── conftest.py
│   ├── test_io/
│   ├── test_geometry/
│   ├── test_viz/
│   └── fixtures/
├── docs/
│   ├── source/
│   └── tutorials/
└── examples/
    ├── basic_visualization.py
    ├── surface_analysis.py
    └── interactive_plotting.py
```

### **4. Core Classes Architecture**

```python
# Core class hierarchy using dataclasses and type hints
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional, Tuple, List
import numpy as np
import trimesh

@dataclass
class SurfaceGeometry:
    """Base surface mesh representation"""
    vertices: np.ndarray  # (n_vertices, 3)
    faces: np.ndarray     # (n_faces, 3)
    hemi: Optional[str] = None  # 'lh' or 'rh'
    
    @property
    def mesh(self) -> trimesh.Trimesh:
        return trimesh.Trimesh(vertices=self.vertices, faces=self.faces)

@dataclass 
class NeuroSurface(SurfaceGeometry):
    """Surface with associated data values"""
    data: np.ndarray      # (n_vertices,) or subset
    indices: Optional[np.ndarray] = None  # vertex indices if data is subset
```

### **5. Implementation Phases**

#### **Phase 1: Core Infrastructure (Week 1-2)**
- Set up project structure
- Implement base classes (SurfaceGeometry, NeuroSurface)
- Basic I/O for FreeSurfer and GIFTI formats using nibabel
- Unit tests for core functionality

#### **Phase 2: Visualization (Week 2-3)**
- Implement view_surface() using vedo
- Port color mapping functionality
- Add support for viewpoints and camera controls
- Create plotly-based interactive visualization

#### **Phase 3: Geometry & Analysis (Week 3-4)**
- Port curvature calculations
- Implement surface smoothing with pymeshlab
- Graph/neighborhood operations with networkx
- Surface searchlight analysis

#### **Phase 4: Advanced Features (Week 4-5)**
- ROI operations
- Volume to surface mapping
- AFNI NIML format support
- Performance optimization

#### **Phase 5: Documentation & Testing (Week 5-6)**
- Convert R vignettes to Jupyter notebooks
- API documentation with Sphinx
- Comprehensive test suite
- Performance benchmarking vs R version

### **6. Key Implementation Examples**

```python
# Example: view_surface replacement using vedo
import vedo
import numpy as np

def view_surface(surf_geom, vals=None, cmap='rainbow', alpha=1.0, 
                 viewpoint='lateral', new_window=True, **kwargs):
    """Display 3D surface using vedo (replaces rgl)"""
    
    # Create vedo mesh
    mesh = vedo.Mesh([surf_geom.vertices, surf_geom.faces])
    
    # Apply vertex colors if values provided
    if vals is not None:
        mesh.cmap(cmap, vals, alpha=alpha)
    
    # Set viewpoint
    viewpoints = {
        'lateral': {'azimuth': 0, 'elevation': 0},
        'medial': {'azimuth': 180, 'elevation': 0},
        'ventral': {'azimuth': 0, 'elevation': -90},
        'posterior': {'azimuth': 90, 'elevation': 0}
    }
    
    # Create plotter and show
    plt = vedo.Plotter(bg='white')
    plt.show(mesh, viewup='z', camera=viewpoints.get(viewpoint))
    
    return plt
```

### **7. Testing Strategy**

- Use pytest for unit tests
- Create R-Python equivalence tests using rpy2 during development
- Golden test fixtures from R outputs
- Property-based testing with hypothesis
- Performance benchmarks with pytest-benchmark

### **8. Migration Guide Components**

Create `MIGRATING_FROM_R.md` with:
- Function mapping table (R → Python)
- Code pattern translations
- Common gotchas (1-based vs 0-based indexing)
- Performance considerations
- Interactive notebook examples

This plan provides a clear path forward for porting neurosurf to Python while maintaining feature parity and leveraging Python's strengths in scientific computing and visualization.

# pyneurosurf

A Python package for surface-based neuroimaging data analysis and visualization.

## Overview

pyneurosurf is a Python port of the R package [neurosurf](https://github.com/bbuchsbaum/neurosurf), providing tools for working with cortical surface meshes in neuroimaging. It offers:

- Loading and saving surface files (FreeSurfer, GIFTI, AFNI NIML formats)
- 3D visualization with interactive controls
- Surface-based analysis (smoothing, searchlight, ROI operations)
- Curvature and geometry calculations
- Integration with standard neuroimaging workflows

## Installation

```bash
pip install pyneurosurf
```

For development installation:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/bbuchsbaum/pyneurosurf.git
cd pyneurosurf
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

## Quick Start

```python
import pyneurosurf as pns

# Load a surface
surf = pns.read_surface("path/to/surface.gii")

# Visualize with data
data = np.random.randn(surf.n_vertices)
pns.view_surface(surf, data=data, cmap='viridis')

# Compute curvature
curv = pns.curvature(surf)

# Surface smoothing
smooth_data = pns.smooth_surface(surf, data, iterations=5)
```

## Features

- **File I/O**: Support for major neuroimaging surface formats
- **Visualization**: Interactive 3D rendering with customizable colormaps
- **Geometry**: Curvature, surface area, and geodesic distance calculations
- **Analysis**: Surface-based searchlight, smoothing, and ROI operations
- **Integration**: Compatible with nibabel, nilearn, and other neuroimaging tools

## Documentation

Full documentation is available at [https://pyneurosurf.readthedocs.io](https://pyneurosurf.readthedocs.io)

## Migration from R

If you're migrating from the R neurosurf package, see our [migration guide](docs/MIGRATING_FROM_R.md) for API mappings and examples.

## License

GPL-2.0 or later

## Citation

If you use pyneurosurf in your research, please cite:

```bibtex
@software{pyneurosurf,
  author = {Buchsbaum, Bradley R.},
  title = {pyneurosurf: Surface-based neuroimaging in Python},
  year = {2024},
  url = {https://github.com/bbuchsbaum/pyneurosurf}
}
```

"""Surface geometry base class and operations."""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Tuple, Union, List
import numpy as np
import trimesh
import networkx as nx
from scipy.spatial import cKDTree


@dataclass
class SurfaceGeometry:
    """
    Base class for representing a triangular mesh surface.
    
    This class encapsulates the geometric properties of a surface mesh,
    including vertices, faces, and optional hemisphere information.
    
    Attributes
    ----------
    vertices : np.ndarray
        Array of shape (n_vertices, 3) containing the 3D coordinates of each vertex.
    faces : np.ndarray
        Array of shape (n_faces, 3) containing the vertex indices for each triangular face.
        Uses 0-based indexing.
    hemi : str, optional
        Hemisphere identifier ('lh' for left, 'rh' for right, or None).
    metadata : dict
        Additional metadata associated with the surface.
    
    Properties
    ----------
    n_vertices : int
        Number of vertices in the surface.
    n_faces : int
        Number of triangular faces in the surface.
    mesh : trimesh.Trimesh
        Trimesh object representation of the surface.
    bounds : tuple
        Bounding box of the surface as ((xmin, ymin, zmin), (xmax, ymax, zmax)).
    center : np.ndarray
        Center point of the surface bounding box.
    
    Examples
    --------
    >>> vertices = np.array([[0, 0, 0], [1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 1]])
    >>> faces = np.array([[0, 1, 2], [0, 1, 3], [0, 2, 3], [1, 2, 3]])
    >>> surf = SurfaceGeometry(vertices, faces, hemi='lh')
    >>> print(f"Surface has {surf.n_vertices} vertices and {surf.n_faces} faces")
    """
    
    vertices: np.ndarray
    faces: np.ndarray
    hemi: Optional[str] = None
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Validate inputs and ensure correct data types."""
        self.vertices = np.asarray(self.vertices, dtype=np.float32)
        self.faces = np.asarray(self.faces, dtype=np.int32)
        
        if self.vertices.ndim != 2 or self.vertices.shape[1] != 3:
            raise ValueError(
                f"vertices must be shape (n_vertices, 3), got {self.vertices.shape}"
            )
        
        if self.faces.ndim != 2 or self.faces.shape[1] != 3:
            raise ValueError(
                f"faces must be shape (n_faces, 3), got {self.faces.shape}"
            )
        
        if self.hemi is not None and self.hemi not in ['lh', 'rh']:
            raise ValueError(f"hemi must be 'lh', 'rh', or None, got {self.hemi}")
        
        # Check face indices are valid
        max_idx = self.faces.max()
        if max_idx >= self.n_vertices:
            raise ValueError(
                f"Face contains vertex index {max_idx} but only "
                f"{self.n_vertices} vertices exist"
            )
        
        if self.faces.min() < 0:
            raise ValueError("Face indices must be non-negative")
    
    @property
    def n_vertices(self) -> int:
        """Number of vertices in the surface."""
        return len(self.vertices)
    
    @property
    def n_faces(self) -> int:
        """Number of faces in the surface."""
        return len(self.faces)
    
    @property
    def mesh(self) -> trimesh.Trimesh:
        """Get trimesh representation of the surface."""
        if not hasattr(self, '_mesh'):
            self._mesh = trimesh.Trimesh(
                vertices=self.vertices,
                faces=self.faces,
                process=False  # Don't modify the mesh
            )
        return self._mesh
    
    @property
    def bounds(self) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """Get bounding box of the surface."""
        return self.vertices.min(axis=0), self.vertices.max(axis=0)
    
    @property
    def center(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get center of the bounding box."""
        min_coords, max_coords = self.bounds
        return (min_coords + max_coords) / 2
    
    @property
    def surface_area(self) -> float:
        """Total surface area of the mesh."""
        return self.mesh.area
    
    @property
    def vertex_normals(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get vertex normals (averaged from adjacent face normals)."""
        return self.mesh.vertex_normals
    
    @property
    def face_normals(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get face normals."""
        return self.mesh.face_normals
    
    def coords(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get vertex coordinates (alias for vertices)."""
        return self.vertices
    
    def nodes(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get vertex indices."""
        return np.arange(self.n_vertices)
    
    def edges(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get unique edges in the mesh."""
        return self.mesh.edges_unique
    
    def vertex_neighbors(self) -> List[List[int]]:
        """
        Get neighboring vertices for each vertex.
        
        Returns
        -------
        list of lists
            For each vertex, a list of indices of neighboring vertices.
        """
        # Build adjacency from faces
        neighbors = [set() for _ in range(self.n_vertices)]
        
        for face in self.faces:
            for i in range(3):
                v1 = face[i]
                v2 = face[(i + 1) % 3]
                neighbors[v1].add(v2)
                neighbors[v2].add(v1)
        
        return [sorted(list(n)) for n in neighbors]
    
    def face_adjacency(self) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """
        Get face adjacency information.
        
        Returns
        -------
        face_adjacency : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_edges, 2) where each row contains indices
            of two faces that share an edge.
        shared_edges : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_edges, 2) containing the vertex indices
            of the shared edge.
        """
        return self.mesh.face_adjacency, self.mesh.face_adjacency_edges
    
    def kdtree(self) -> cKDTree:
        """
        Build a KD-tree for efficient spatial queries.
        
        Returns
        -------
        cKDTree
            Spatial index of vertex positions.
        """
        if not hasattr(self, '_kdtree'):
            self._kdtree = cKDTree(self.vertices)
        return self._kdtree
    
    def nearest_vertex(self, points: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """
        Find nearest vertex for each query point.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        points : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_points, 3) containing query points.
        
        Returns
        -------
        distances : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_points,) containing distances to nearest vertex.
        indices : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_points,) containing indices of nearest vertices.
        """
        points = np.asarray(points).reshape(-1, 3)
        distances, indices = self.kdtree().query(points)
        return distances, indices
    
    def to_graph(self) -> nx.Graph:
        """
        Convert surface to NetworkX graph.
        
        Returns
        -------
        nx.Graph
            Graph where nodes are vertices and edges connect adjacent vertices.
            Node attributes include 'pos' (3D coordinates).
            Edge attributes include 'length' (Euclidean distance).
        """
        G = nx.Graph()
        
        # Add nodes with positions
        for i, pos in enumerate(self.vertices):
            G.add_node(i, pos=pos)
        
        # Add edges from faces
        for face in self.faces:
            for i in range(3):
                v1, v2 = face[i], face[(i + 1) % 3]
                if not G.has_edge(v1, v2):
                    length = np.linalg.norm(self.vertices[v1] - self.vertices[v2])
                    G.add_edge(v1, v2, length=length)
        
        return G
    
    def transform(self, matrix: np.ndarray) -> 'SurfaceGeometry':
        """
        Apply 4x4 transformation matrix to the surface.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        matrix : np.ndarray
            4x4 transformation matrix.
        
        Returns
        -------
        SurfaceGeometry
            New surface with transformed coordinates.
        """
        if matrix.shape != (4, 4):
            raise ValueError(f"Transformation matrix must be 4x4, got {matrix.shape}")
        
        # Convert to homogeneous coordinates
        coords_h = np.hstack([self.vertices, np.ones((self.n_vertices, 1))])
        
        # Apply transformation
        transformed = coords_h @ matrix.T
        
        # Convert back to 3D
        new_vertices = transformed[:, :3] / transformed[:, 3:4]
        
        return SurfaceGeometry(
            vertices=new_vertices,
            faces=self.faces.copy(),
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata=self.metadata.copy()
        )
    
    def extract_component(self, vertex_indices: np.ndarray) -> 'SurfaceGeometry':
        """
        Extract a connected component of the surface.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        vertex_indices : np.ndarray
            Indices of vertices to include in the component.
        
        Returns
        -------
        SurfaceGeometry
            New surface containing only the specified vertices and
            faces that are fully contained within the vertex set.
        """
        vertex_indices = np.asarray(vertex_indices)
        vertex_mask = np.zeros(self.n_vertices, dtype=bool)
        vertex_mask[vertex_indices] = True
        
        # Find faces where all vertices are in the selection
        face_mask = np.all(vertex_mask[self.faces], axis=1)
        selected_faces = self.faces[face_mask]
        
        # Create vertex mapping
        new_indices = np.full(self.n_vertices, -1, dtype=int)
        new_indices[vertex_indices] = np.arange(len(vertex_indices))
        
        # Remap face indices
        new_faces = new_indices[selected_faces]
        new_vertices = self.vertices[vertex_indices]
        
        return SurfaceGeometry(
            vertices=new_vertices,
            faces=new_faces,
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata=self.metadata.copy()
        )
    
    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        """String representation of the surface."""

"""Surface geometry base class and operations."""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Tuple, Union, List
import numpy as np
import trimesh
import networkx as nx
from scipy.spatial import cKDTree


@dataclass
class SurfaceGeometry:
    """
    Base class for representing a triangular mesh surface.
    
    This class encapsulates the geometric properties of a surface mesh,
    including vertices, faces, and optional hemisphere information.
    
    Attributes
    ----------
    vertices : np.ndarray
        Array of shape (n_vertices, 3) containing the 3D coordinates of each vertex.
    faces : np.ndarray
        Array of shape (n_faces, 3) containing the vertex indices for each triangular face.
        Uses 0-based indexing.
    hemi : str, optional
        Hemisphere identifier ('lh' for left, 'rh' for right, or None).
    metadata : dict
        Additional metadata associated with the surface.
    
    Properties
    ----------
    n_vertices : int
        Number of vertices in the surface.
    n_faces : int
        Number of triangular faces in the surface.
    mesh : trimesh.Trimesh
        Trimesh object representation of the surface.
    bounds : tuple
        Bounding box of the surface as ((xmin, ymin, zmin), (xmax, ymax, zmax)).
    center : np.ndarray
        Center point of the surface bounding box.
    
    Examples
    --------
    >>> vertices = np.array([[0, 0, 0], [1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 1]])
    >>> faces = np.array([[0, 1, 2], [0, 1, 3], [0, 2, 3], [1, 2, 3]])
    >>> surf = SurfaceGeometry(vertices, faces, hemi='lh')
    >>> print(f"Surface has {surf.n_vertices} vertices and {surf.n_faces} faces")
    """
    
    vertices: np.ndarray
    faces: np.ndarray
    hemi: Optional[str] = None
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Validate inputs and ensure correct data types."""
        self.vertices = np.asarray(self.vertices, dtype=np.float32)
        self.faces = np.asarray(self.faces, dtype=np.int32)
        
        if self.vertices.ndim != 2 or self.vertices.shape[1] != 3:
            raise ValueError(
                f"vertices must be shape (n_vertices, 3), got {self.vertices.shape}"
            )
        
        if self.faces.ndim != 2 or self.faces.shape[1] != 3:
            raise ValueError(
                f"faces must be shape (n_faces, 3), got {self.faces.shape}"
            )
        
        if self.hemi is not None and self.hemi not in ['lh', 'rh']:
            raise ValueError(f"hemi must be 'lh', 'rh', or None, got {self.hemi}")
        
        # Check face indices are valid
        max_idx = self.faces.max()
        if max_idx >= self.n_vertices:
            raise ValueError(
                f"Face contains vertex index {max_idx} but only "
                f"{self.n_vertices} vertices exist"
            )
        
        if self.faces.min() < 0:
            raise ValueError("Face indices must be non-negative")
    
    @property
    def n_vertices(self) -> int:
        """Number of vertices in the surface."""
        return len(self.vertices)
    
    @property
    def n_faces(self) -> int:
        """Number of faces in the surface."""
        return len(self.faces)
    
    @property
    def mesh(self) -> trimesh.Trimesh:
        """Get trimesh representation of the surface."""
        if not hasattr(self, '_mesh'):
            self._mesh = trimesh.Trimesh(
                vertices=self.vertices,
                faces=self.faces,
                process=False  # Don't modify the mesh
            )
        return self._mesh
    
    @property
    def bounds(self) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """Get bounding box of the surface."""
        return self.vertices.min(axis=0), self.vertices.max(axis=0)
    
    @property
    def center(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get center of the bounding box."""
        min_coords, max_coords = self.bounds
        return (min_coords + max_coords) / 2
    
    @property
    def surface_area(self) -> float:
        """Total surface area of the mesh."""
        return self.mesh.area
    
    @property
    def vertex_normals(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get vertex normals (averaged from adjacent face normals)."""
        return self.mesh.vertex_normals
    
    @property
    def face_normals(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get face normals."""
        return self.mesh.face_normals
    
    def coords(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get vertex coordinates (alias for vertices)."""
        return self.vertices
    
    def nodes(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get vertex indices."""
        return np.arange(self.n_vertices)
    
    def edges(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Get unique edges in the mesh."""
        return self.mesh.edges_unique
    
    def vertex_neighbors(self) -> List[List[int]]:
        """
        Get neighboring vertices for each vertex.
        
        Returns
        -------
        list of lists
            For each vertex, a list of indices of neighboring vertices.
        """
        # Build adjacency from faces
        neighbors = [set() for _ in range(self.n_vertices)]
        
        for face in self.faces:
            for i in range(3):
                v1 = face[i]
                v2 = face[(i + 1) % 3]
                neighbors[v1].add(v2)
                neighbors[v2].add(v1)
        
        return [sorted(list(n)) for n in neighbors]
    
    def face_adjacency(self) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """
        Get face adjacency information.
        
        Returns
        -------
        face_adjacency : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_edges, 2) where each row contains indices
            of two faces that share an edge.
        shared_edges : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_edges, 2) containing the vertex indices
            of the shared edge.
        """
        return self.mesh.face_adjacency, self.mesh.face_adjacency_edges
    
    def kdtree(self) -> cKDTree:
        """
        Build a KD-tree for efficient spatial queries.
        
        Returns
        -------
        cKDTree
            Spatial index of vertex positions.
        """
        if not hasattr(self, '_kdtree'):
            self._kdtree = cKDTree(self.vertices)
        return self._kdtree
    
    def nearest_vertex(self, points: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """
        Find nearest vertex for each query point.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        points : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_points, 3) containing query points.
        
        Returns
        -------
        distances : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_points,) containing distances to nearest vertex.
        indices : np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_points,) containing indices of nearest vertices.
        """
        points = np.asarray(points).reshape(-1, 3)
        distances, indices = self.kdtree().query(points)
        return distances, indices
    
    def to_graph(self) -> nx.Graph:
        """
        Convert surface to NetworkX graph.
        
        Returns
        -------
        nx.Graph
            Graph where nodes are vertices and edges connect adjacent vertices.
            Node attributes include 'pos' (3D coordinates).
            Edge attributes include 'length' (Euclidean distance).
        """
        G = nx.Graph()
        
        # Add nodes with positions
        for i, pos in enumerate(self.vertices):
            G.add_node(i, pos=pos)
        
        # Add edges from faces
        for face in self.faces:
            for i in range(3):
                v1, v2 = face[i], face[(i + 1) % 3]
                if not G.has_edge(v1, v2):
                    length = np.linalg.norm(self.vertices[v1] - self.vertices[v2])
                    G.add_edge(v1, v2, length=length)
        
        return G
    
    def transform(self, matrix: np.ndarray) -> 'SurfaceGeometry':
        """
        Apply 4x4 transformation matrix to the surface.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        matrix : np.ndarray
            4x4 transformation matrix.
        
        Returns
        -------
        SurfaceGeometry
            New surface with transformed coordinates.
        """
        if matrix.shape != (4, 4):
            raise ValueError(f"Transformation matrix must be 4x4, got {matrix.shape}")
        
        # Convert to homogeneous coordinates
        coords_h = np.hstack([self.vertices, np.ones((self.n_vertices, 1))])
        
        # Apply transformation
        transformed = coords_h @ matrix.T
        
        # Convert back to 3D
        new_vertices = transformed[:, :3] / transformed[:, 3:4]
        
        return SurfaceGeometry(
            vertices=new_vertices,
            faces=self.faces.copy(),
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata=self.metadata.copy()
        )
    
    def extract_component(self, vertex_indices: np.ndarray) -> 'SurfaceGeometry':
        """
        Extract a connected component of the surface.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        vertex_indices : np.ndarray
            Indices of vertices to include in the component.
        
        Returns
        -------
        SurfaceGeometry
            New surface containing only the specified vertices and
            faces that are fully contained within the vertex set.
        """
        vertex_indices = np.asarray(vertex_indices)
        vertex_mask = np.zeros(self.n_vertices, dtype=bool)
        vertex_mask[vertex_indices] = True
        
        # Find faces where all vertices are in the selection
        face_mask = np.all(vertex_mask[self.faces], axis=1)
        selected_faces = self.faces[face_mask]
        
        # Create vertex mapping
        new_indices = np.full(self.n_vertices, -1, dtype=int)
        new_indices[vertex_indices] = np.arange(len(vertex_indices))
        
        # Remap face indices
        new_faces = new_indices[selected_faces]
        new_vertices = self.vertices[vertex_indices]
        
        return SurfaceGeometry(
            vertices=new_vertices,
            faces=new_faces,
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata=self.metadata.copy()
        )
    
    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        """String representation of the surface."""
        hemi_str = f", hemi='{self.hemi}'" if self.hemi else ""
        return (
            f"SurfaceGeometry(n_vertices={self.n_vertices}, "
            f"n_faces={self.n_faces}{hemi_str})"
        )

"""Surface classes with associated data values."""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Union, List, Dict, Any
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import cm
from matplotlib.colors import Normalize, ListedColormap

from .geometry import SurfaceGeometry


@dataclass
class NeuroSurface(SurfaceGeometry):
    """
    Surface geometry with associated data values.
    
    This class extends SurfaceGeometry to include data values associated
    with vertices, enabling visualization and analysis of surface-based data.
    
    Attributes
    ----------
    vertices : np.ndarray
        Inherited from SurfaceGeometry.
    faces : np.ndarray
        Inherited from SurfaceGeometry.
    data : np.ndarray
        Data values associated with vertices. Can be full length (n_vertices,)
        or a subset if indices are provided.
    indices : np.ndarray, optional
        Vertex indices where data is defined. If None, data is assumed to
        cover all vertices.
    hemi : str, optional
        Inherited from SurfaceGeometry.
    metadata : dict
        Inherited from SurfaceGeometry.
    
    Examples
    --------
    >>> # Create surface with data at all vertices
    >>> surf = NeuroSurface(vertices, faces, data=np.random.randn(n_vertices))
    >>> 
    >>> # Create surface with data at specific vertices
    >>> roi_indices = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40])
    >>> roi_data = np.array([1.5, 2.0, -0.5, 1.0])
    >>> surf = NeuroSurface(vertices, faces, data=roi_data, indices=roi_indices)
    """
    
    data: np.ndarray = field(default_factory=lambda: np.array([]))
    indices: Optional[np.ndarray] = None
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Validate data dimensions."""
        super().__post_init__()
        
        self.data = np.asarray(self.data)
        
        if self.indices is not None:
            self.indices = np.asarray(self.indices, dtype=np.int32)
            if len(self.data) != len(self.indices):
                raise ValueError(
                    f"data length ({len(self.data)}) must match "
                    f"indices length ({len(self.indices)})"
                )
            if self.indices.max() >= self.n_vertices:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"Index {self.indices.max()} exceeds number of vertices "
                    f"({self.n_vertices})"
                )
        else:
            # If no indices, data should cover all vertices
            if len(self.data) > 0 and len(self.data) != self.n_vertices:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"Without indices, data length ({len(self.data)}) must "
                    f"match number of vertices ({self.n_vertices})"
                )
    
    @property
    def data_range(self) -> tuple:
        """Get min and max of data values."""
        if len(self.data) == 0:
            return (np.nan, np.nan)
        return float(np.nanmin(self.data)), float(np.nanmax(self.data))
    
    def get_vertex_data(self, fill_value: float = np.nan) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Get data values for all vertices.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        fill_value : float
            Value to use for vertices without data.
        
        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_vertices,) with data values.
        """
        if self.indices is None:
            return self.data.copy()
        
        # Fill array with default value
        full_data = np.full(self.n_vertices, fill_value, dtype=self.data.dtype)
        full_data[self.indices] = self.data
        return full_data
    
    def threshold(
        self,
        lower: Optional[float] = None,
        upper: Optional[float] = None
    ) -> 'NeuroSurface':
        """
        Create new surface with thresholded data.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        lower : float, optional
            Lower threshold. Values below this are excluded.
        upper : float, optional
            Upper threshold. Values above this are excluded.
        
        Returns
        -------
        NeuroSurface
            New surface with only data values within threshold range.
        """
        mask = np.ones(len(self.data), dtype=bool)
        
        if lower is not None:
            mask &= self.data >= lower
        if upper is not None:
            mask &= self.data <= upper
        
        if self.indices is not None:
            new_indices = self.indices[mask]
        else:
            new_indices = np.where(mask)[0]
        
        return NeuroSurface(
            vertices=self.vertices,
            faces=self.faces,
            data=self.data[mask],
            indices=new_indices,
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata=self.metadata.copy()
        )


@dataclass
class ColorMappedNeuroSurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface with color-mapped data values.
    
    This class extends NeuroSurface to include color mapping functionality,
    allowing data values to be mapped to colors for visualization.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    cmap : str or Colormap
        Colormap for mapping data values to colors.
    norm : Normalize
        Normalization for data to colormap mapping.
    alpha : float
        Transparency value (0-1).
    """
    
    cmap: Union[str, ListedColormap] = 'viridis'
    norm: Optional[Normalize] = None
    alpha: float = 1.0
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Initialize colormap and normalization."""
        super().__post_init__()
        
        # Set up colormap
        if isinstance(self.cmap, str):
            self.cmap = cm.get_cmap(self.cmap)
        
        # Set up normalization if not provided
        if self.norm is None and len(self.data) > 0:
            vmin, vmax = self.data_range
            if not np.isnan(vmin):
                self.norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
    
    def get_colors(self, fill_color: str = '#808080') -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Get RGBA colors for all vertices.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        fill_color : str
            Hex color for vertices without data.
        
        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_vertices, 4) with RGBA values.
        """
        # Initialize with fill color
        from matplotlib.colors import to_rgba
        colors = np.tile(to_rgba(fill_color), (self.n_vertices, 1))
        
        if len(self.data) > 0 and self.norm is not None:
            # Map data to colors
            mapped_colors = self.cmap(self.norm(self.data))
            
            if self.indices is not None:
                colors[self.indices] = mapped_colors
            else:
                colors = mapped_colors
        
        # Apply alpha
        colors[:, 3] *= self.alpha
        
        return colors
    
    def update_colormap(
        self,
        cmap: Optional[Union[str, ListedColormap]] = None,
        vmin: Optional[float] = None,
        vmax: Optional[float] = None
    ) -> None:
        """
        Update colormap and/or normalization.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        cmap : str or Colormap, optional
            New colormap.
        vmin : float, optional
            New minimum value for normalization.
        vmax : float, optional
            New maximum value for normalization.
        """
        if cmap is not None:
            if isinstance(cmap, str):
                self.cmap = cm.get_cmap(cmap)
            else:
                self.cmap = cmap
        
        if vmin is not None or vmax is not None:
            current_vmin, current_vmax = self.data_range
            vmin = vmin if vmin is not None else current_vmin
            vmax = vmax if vmax is not None else current_vmax
            self.norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)


@dataclass
class VertexColoredNeuroSurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface with explicit vertex colors.
    
    This class allows direct specification of colors for each vertex,
    useful for categorical data or pre-computed color mappings.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    colors : np.ndarray
        Array of shape (n_vertices, 3) or (n_vertices, 4) containing
        RGB or RGBA values for each vertex.
    """
    
    colors: np.ndarray = field(default_factory=lambda: np.array([]))
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Validate color array."""
        super().__post_init__()
        
        if len(self.colors) > 0:
            self.colors = np.asarray(self.colors)
            
            # Validate shape
            if self.colors.ndim != 2 or self.colors.shape[1] not in [3, 4]:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"colors must be shape (n_vertices, 3) or (n_vertices, 4), "
                    f"got {self.colors.shape}"
                )
            
            # Add alpha channel if needed
            if self.colors.shape[1] == 3:
                alpha_channel = np.ones((len(self.colors), 1))
                self.colors = np.hstack([self.colors, alpha_channel])
            
            # Validate color values
            if self.colors.max() > 1.0 and self.colors.max() <= 255:
                # Convert from 0-255 to 0-1 range
                self.colors = self.colors / 255.0
            elif self.colors.max() > 255:
                raise ValueError("Color values must be in range 0-1 or 0-255")


@dataclass
class LabeledNeuroSurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface with labeled regions.
    
    This class is designed for parcellated surfaces where vertices
    are assigned to discrete labels/regions.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    labels : np.ndarray
        Integer labels for each vertex or subset.
    label_names : dict
        Mapping from label integers to label names.
    label_colors : dict
        Mapping from label integers to colors.
    """
    
    labels: np.ndarray = field(default_factory=lambda: np.array([], dtype=int))
    label_names: Dict[int, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
    label_colors: Dict[int, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Set data from labels and validate."""
        # Set data to be the labels
        if len(self.labels) > 0:
            self.data = self.labels.astype(float)
        
        super().__post_init__()
        
        # Generate default colors if not provided
        if len(self.labels) > 0 and not self.label_colors:
            unique_labels = np.unique(self.labels)
            n_labels = len(unique_labels)
            
            # Use a categorical colormap
            colors = cm.get_cmap('tab20', n_labels)
            
            for i, label in enumerate(unique_labels):
                if label not in self.label_colors:
                    self.label_colors[label] = colors(i)
    
    def get_label_surface(self, label: Union[int, str]) -> 'NeuroSurface':
        """
        Extract surface for a specific label.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        label : int or str
            Label value or name.
        
        Returns
        -------
        NeuroSurface
            Surface containing only vertices with the specified label.
        """
        if isinstance(label, str):
            # Find label value from name
            label_val = None
            for val, name in self.label_names.items():
                if name == label:
                    label_val = val
                    break
            if label_val is None:
                raise ValueError(f"Label name '{label}' not found")
            label = label_val
        
        # Find vertices with this label
        if self.indices is not None:
            mask = self.labels == label
            selected_indices = self.indices[mask]
            selected_data = self.data[mask]
        else:
            selected_indices = np.where(self.labels == label)[0]
            selected_data = self.data[selected_indices]
        
        return NeuroSurface(
            vertices=self.vertices,
            faces=self.faces,
            data=selected_data,
            indices=selected_indices,
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata={**self.metadata, 'label': label}
        )


@dataclass
class ROISurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface representing a region of interest (ROI).
    
    This class is optimized for working with surface patches or ROIs,
    providing additional functionality for ROI-based analysis.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    roi_name : str
        Name of the ROI.
    source_indices : np.ndarray
        Indices in the original full surface.
    """
    
    roi_name: str = "unnamed_roi"
    source_indices: Optional[np.ndarray] = None
    
    def to_full_surface(
        self,
        full_geometry: SurfaceGeometry,
        fill_value: float = 0.0
    ) -> NeuroSurface:
        """
        Project ROI data back to full surface.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        full_geometry : SurfaceGeometry
            The full surface geometry.
        fill_value : float
            Value for vertices outside the ROI.
        
        Returns
        -------
        NeuroSurface
            Full surface with ROI data.
        """
        if self.source_indices is None:
            raise ValueError("source_indices required for projection to full surface")
        
        # Create full data array
        full_data = np.full(full_geometry.n_vertices, fill_value)
        full_data[self.source_indices] = self.data
        
        return NeuroSurface(
            vertices=full_geometry.vertices,
            faces=full_geometry.faces,
            data=full_data,
            hemi=full_geometry.hemi,
            metadata={**full_geometry.metadata, 'roi_name': self.roi_name}
        )

        else:
            # If no indices, data should cover all vertices
            if len(self.data) > 0 and len(self.data) != self.n_vertices:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"Without indices, data length ({len(self.data)}) must "
                    f"match number of vertices ({self.n_vertices})"
                )
    
    @property
    def data_range(self) -> tuple:
        """Get min and max of data values."""
        if len(self.data) == 0:
            return (np.nan, np.nan)
        return float(np.nanmin(self.data)), float(np.nanmax(self.data))
    
    def get_vertex_data(self, fill_value: float = np.nan) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Get data values for all vertices.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        fill_value : float
            Value to use for vertices without data.
        
        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_vertices,) with data values.
        """
        if self.indices is None:
            return self.data.copy()
        
        # Fill array with default value
        full_data = np.full(self.n_vertices, fill_value, dtype=self.data.dtype)
        full_data[self.indices] = self.data
        return full_data
    
    def threshold(
        self,
        lower: Optional[float] = None,
        upper: Optional[float] = None
    ) -> 'NeuroSurface':
        """
        Create new surface with thresholded data.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        lower : float, optional
            Lower threshold. Values below this are excluded.
        upper : float, optional
            Upper threshold. Values above this are excluded.
        
        Returns
        -------
        NeuroSurface
            New surface with only data values within threshold range.
        """
        mask = np.ones(len(self.data), dtype=bool)
        
        if lower is not None:
            mask &= self.data >= lower
        if upper is not None:
            mask &= self.data <= upper
        
        if self.indices is not None:
            new_indices = self.indices[mask]
        else:
            new_indices = np.where(mask)[0]
        
        return NeuroSurface(
            vertices=self.vertices,
            faces=self.faces,
            data=self.data[mask],
            indices=new_indices,
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata=self.metadata.copy()
        )


@dataclass
class ColorMappedNeuroSurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface with color-mapped data values.
    
    This class extends NeuroSurface to include color mapping functionality,
    allowing data values to be mapped to colors for visualization.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    cmap : str or Colormap
        Colormap for mapping data values to colors.
    norm : Normalize
        Normalization for data to colormap mapping.
    alpha : float
        Transparency value (0-1).
    """
    
    cmap: Union[str, ListedColormap] = 'viridis'
    norm: Optional[Normalize] = None
    alpha: float = 1.0
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Initialize colormap and normalization."""
        super().__post_init__()
        
        # Set up colormap
        if isinstance(self.cmap, str):
            self.cmap = cm.get_cmap(self.cmap)
        
        # Set up normalization if not provided
        if self.norm is None and len(self.data) > 0:
            vmin, vmax = self.data_range
            if not np.isnan(vmin):
                self.norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
    
    def get_colors(self, fill_color: str = '#808080') -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Get RGBA colors for all vertices.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        fill_color : str
            Hex color for vertices without data.
        
        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_vertices, 4) with RGBA values.
        """
        # Initialize with fill color
        from matplotlib.colors import to_rgba
        colors = np.tile(to_rgba(fill_color), (self.n_vertices, 1))
        
        if len(self.data) > 0 and self.norm is not None:
            # Map data to colors
            mapped_colors = self.cmap(self.norm(self.data))
            
            if self.indices is not None:
                colors[self.indices] = mapped_colors
            else:
                colors = mapped_colors
        
        # Apply alpha
        colors[:, 3] *= self.alpha
        
        return colors
    
    def update_colormap(
        self,
        cmap: Optional[Union[str, ListedColormap]] = None,
        vmin: Optional[float] = None,
        vmax: Optional[float] = None
    ) -> None:
        """
        Update colormap and/or normalization.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        cmap : str or Colormap, optional
            New colormap.
        vmin : float, optional
            New minimum value for normalization.
        vmax : float, optional
            New maximum value for normalization.
        """
        if cmap is not None:
            if isinstance(cmap, str):
                self.cmap = cm.get_cmap(cmap)
            else:
                self.cmap = cmap
        
        if vmin is not None or vmax is not None:
            current_vmin, current_vmax = self.data_range
            vmin = vmin if vmin is not None else current_vmin
            vmax = vmax if vmax is not None else current_vmax
            self.norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)


@dataclass
class VertexColoredNeuroSurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface with explicit vertex colors.
    
    This class allows direct specification of colors for each vertex,
    useful for categorical data or pre-computed color mappings.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    colors : np.ndarray
        Array of shape (n_vertices, 3) or (n_vertices, 4) containing
        RGB or RGBA values for each vertex.
    """
    
    colors: np.ndarray = field(default_factory=lambda: np.array([]))
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Validate color array."""
        super().__post_init__()
        
        if len(self.colors) > 0:
            self.colors = np.asarray(self.colors)
            
            # Validate shape
            if self.colors.ndim != 2 or self.colors.shape[1] not in [3, 4]:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"colors must be shape (n_vertices, 3) or (n_vertices, 4), "
                    f"got {self.colors.shape}"
                )
            
            # Add alpha channel if needed
            if self.colors.shape[1] == 3:
                alpha_channel = np.ones((len(self.colors), 1))
                self.colors = np.hstack([self.colors, alpha_channel])
            
            # Validate color values
            if self.colors.max() > 1.0 and self.colors.max() <= 255:
                # Convert from 0-255 to 0-1 range
                self.colors = self.colors / 255.0
            elif self.colors.max() > 255:
                raise ValueError("Color values must be in range 0-1 or 0-255")


@dataclass
class LabeledNeuroSurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface with labeled regions.
    
    This class is designed for parcellated surfaces where vertices
    are assigned to discrete labels/regions.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    labels : np.ndarray
        Integer labels for each vertex or subset.
    label_names : dict
        Mapping from label integers to label names.
    label_colors : dict
        Mapping from label integers to colors.
    """
    
    labels: np.ndarray = field(default_factory=lambda: np.array([], dtype=int))
    label_names: Dict[int, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
    label_colors: Dict[int, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
    
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Set data from labels and validate."""
        # Set data to be the labels
        if len(self.labels) > 0:
            self.data = self.labels.astype(float)
        
        super().__post_init__()
        
        # Generate default colors if not provided
        if len(self.labels) > 0 and not self.label_colors:
            unique_labels = np.unique(self.labels)
            n_labels = len(unique_labels)
            
            # Use a categorical colormap
            colors = cm.get_cmap('tab20', n_labels)
            
            for i, label in enumerate(unique_labels):
                if label not in self.label_colors:
                    self.label_colors[label] = colors(i)
    
    def get_label_surface(self, label: Union[int, str]) -> 'NeuroSurface':
        """
        Extract surface for a specific label.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        label : int or str
            Label value or name.
        
        Returns
        -------
        NeuroSurface
            Surface containing only vertices with the specified label.
        """
        if isinstance(label, str):
            # Find label value from name
            label_val = None
            for val, name in self.label_names.items():
                if name == label:
                    label_val = val
                    break
            if label_val is None:
                raise ValueError(f"Label name '{label}' not found")
            label = label_val
        
        # Find vertices with this label
        if self.indices is not None:
            mask = self.labels == label
            selected_indices = self.indices[mask]
            selected_data = self.data[mask]
        else:
            selected_indices = np.where(self.labels == label)[0]
            selected_data = self.data[selected_indices]
        
        return NeuroSurface(
            vertices=self.vertices,
            faces=self.faces,
            data=selected_data,
            indices=selected_indices,
            hemi=self.hemi,
            metadata={**self.metadata, 'label': label}
        )


@dataclass
class ROISurface(NeuroSurface):
    """
    Surface representing a region of interest (ROI).
    
    This class is optimized for working with surface patches or ROIs,
    providing additional functionality for ROI-based analysis.
    
    Additional Attributes
    ---------------------
    roi_name : str
        Name of the ROI.
    source_indices : np.ndarray
        Indices in the original full surface.
    """
    
    roi_name: str = "unnamed_roi"
    source_indices: Optional[np.ndarray] = None
    
    def to_full_surface(
        self,
        full_geometry: SurfaceGeometry,
        fill_value: float = 0.0
    ) -> NeuroSurface:
        """
        Project ROI data back to full surface.
        
        Parameters
        ----------
        full_geometry : SurfaceGeometry
            The full surface geometry.
        fill_value : float
            Value for vertices outside the ROI.
        
        Returns
        -------
        NeuroSurface
            Full surface with ROI data.
        """
        if self.source_indices is None:
            raise ValueError("source_indices required for projection to full surface")
        
        # Create full data array
        full_data = np.full(full_geometry.n_vertices, fill_value)
        full_data[self.source_indices] = self.data
        
        return NeuroSurface(
            vertices=full_geometry.vertices,
            faces=full_geometry.faces,
            data=full_data,
            hemi=full_geometry.hemi,
            metadata={**full_geometry.metadata, 'roi_name': self.roi_name}
        )
